sentences.text: Yes.
He-- he-- he was interrogated first.
And I told you what they did to him.
But whatever, you know, questions they asked was first in German to an interpreter, so he had a little time to give the answer.
Not only that they gave-- that they gave him the proper answer, they had typed everything on a piece of paper, on a report.
So when I was called in on the-- [CLEARS THROAT] and they started to interrogate me.
They asked me, naturally, the same question-- if I was Jewish, and all that.
And then they sat me down right in front of the table.
And I-- I-- I knew how to read German and all that.
So when they asked me, I could read every-- all answers of my brother's.
Whatever answer he gave, I had-- I had the same answer.
So they really couldn't-- sometimes, they tried to question another question, but I still had a pretty good idea what-- what was up to.
So I gave them almost the same answers, then they-- then they questioned my brother.
So from then, after the interrogation, we all were put again in a truck and shipped to Bordeaux.
Bordeaux-- it wasn't far away from Bordeaux, France.
And that's on the Southern part of France as well?
Yes.
Mm-hm.
Yeah.
Mm-hm.
That's where the Bordeaux wine comes from.
There, we were put in a military camp.
It was not a regular prison, it was a camp-- a military camp.
And there, they put all the people-- you know, saboteur, people in France who did sabotage, or young people, from 17, 18 years old.
They would put them in prison, because they needed workers in-- in Germany, so they imprisoned you there.
No, I don't.
I don't remember that name.
[COUGHS] And then, when we got in there, we all-- and one thing about the Germans, they didn't want-- wanted to make sure you're always clean.

They adloused-- you know, they took your-- your clothes off, and put it in a cleaning process, like a cleaning machine, and cleaned everything, so that you had nothing to carry with you, lice, or anything like that.
But before they did-- when they did that, they had us take off all our-- all our clothes and just left us in underpants.
And before I left [COUGHS] my mother, my mother had sewed money and the addresses from all our relatives from-- from the United States in my pants, here in the-- in the band, you know?
In the band.
[COUGHS] Excuse me.
So-- so I-- I was getting worried.
What will happen if I leave that money and the addresses in the pants?
And what will happen to them?
So I had tried with my teeth to open it up-- the-- the-- the thread.
And opened up the thread, and took out the money.
And I took out the addresses.
And the first thing I did with the addresses, I ripped them in 100 pieces and swallowed them, because the Germans were known that they can put paper together and find out what's behind it.
How did you know that?
How did you know all that?
You get to know all these things fast.
[LAUGHS]
With some of them.
With some of them.
They-- they all said, be careful with-- with the Germans.
Don't have anything on you what could lead to something else.
So that's why I took the papers, ripped them apart, and swallowed them.
Then I took the money I had and put it under the mattress where we-- in the bunk beds where we were sleeping in.
And I-- I-- we had an informer in there.
We were 24 people.
And the Germans always had somebody to catch somebody.
He looked exactly like everybody else, and he was an informer.
No.
No.
No.
No, we never could-- were able to find out.
So he was an informer.
And about-- after my clothes were gone, about 15, 20 minutes later, two SS guys walk into the-- to the hall, went directly to my bed, put their hands under the mattress, and found the money.
Because they were afraid we would flee or-- so we had money.
So they-- they-- when the clothes came back, they called me into the office, and they put me in solitary.
You know what solitary is?
The solitary confinement?
Confinement Solitary confinement.
I was there for two weeks.
All I saw, a little light, a hole in the door where they pushed in my food, if they gave me some.
And then you had a little water and like a toilet.
In France, they didn't have seat toilet, you have just to stand on.
And then you have a hole in the ground when you-- when doing your business.
I was in there for two weeks, in-- in confinement.
After two weeks, they sent all the people they had in that camp to Paris.
So they put us all in boxcar-- in big boxcars, 60 to 65 people in a boxcar.
And-- and when my brother-- I got together again with my brother.
On-- on that boxcar, into the boxcar.
Yeah.
Yeah.
A train, yeah.
Uh-huh, on a train.
Uh-huh.
They were all big boxcars to transport the prisoners, you know, into another camp.
When my brother looked at me, I said, hi.
Hi, Alex.
He looked at me, he didn't recognize me, you know, being white and all wrinkled up and everything.
Mm-mm.
Nobody knowed anything, what the Germans was going to do.
No explanation.
Yes, we were-- because we crossed into-- into Spain.
Yeah.
Uh-huh.
Yeah.
But the others were detained, because they were doing some kind of sabotage, or did something, or somebody got killed, a German got killed.
They would go on a rampage and arrest 100 young people, just to-- to make sure that nothing happens to them in-- in-- in France.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Pardon me?
That was in-- let's see, that was in February or March of 1943.
Mm-hm.
You just had your-- your clothes.
That's all you had.
And-- and washing?
Soap?
There was not such things.
You washed with water, kept yourself-- the only thing we have to do-- kept yourself clean with water.
From France, we were again assembled and put into a train.
But where the camp was, to go to the train station, we had to walk about a mile.
And before we left the camp, I had written on a little piece of paper the address for the people where my parents were, from [NON-ENGLISH].
And as we marched to the station, I found-- I found a little stone on the street.
Put that paper around the stone, and threw that paper over a wall in somebody's yard.
And it-- and on the paper was saying, we are leaving for-- no destination.
That's all we said, and on these things.
These people found that stone and wrote a beautiful note to my friends that they-- that we are still alive and that we are on the transport.
They don't know where, but least they know that we are still living.
I mean, any-- anybody-- I mean, not everybody does that-- just find the note, and to-- to notify that-- that you are still alive.
So then, we were put in, and again, in boxcars on a train of about-- I would say, they transported that time 1,500 people to Germany at a time.
That's how many people were in that camp.
And were-- what were conditions like in that train?
On that train?
Was it-- was it different from the first one that--
No, no difference.
There was a little straw on the floor where we could sit down.
And can you imagine having 60 peoples in a boxcar?
A boxcar is the size of this room, a little larger, and having people sitting there.
And in the middle, they had a big drum where you have to go and urinate, or if you have to do something else.
And then, at night, they would dump it someplace.
And you would step over people, in order to get to the boxcar.
They would give you a piece of bread like this.
And-- and they would have a bucket of water in there.
And everybody drank out of the bucket of water.
And we arrived at Buchenwald, after two and a half days, almost three days.
In Buchenwald, naturally, we were taken from the station and bussed-- not on buses-- on-- on trucks into the Camp Buchenwald.
And there, we were again stripped from head to toe, shaven from head-- all over, from head to toe, shaven.
And then we had to go and take a shower.
And then you had to go through a line where you would get your nice gray and blue uniform, the prisoner's uniform.
And then you would go and get your number.
And then you were put in barracks.
And from there, they assigned you every day a different jobs.

I-- I had to go and work in the-- in the stone mill, clearing stones to be grinded, and-- and then other stones to be going into the camp to build streets, and stuff like that, in the-- in Buchenwald.
And there, we were about three weeks, in Buchenwald.
From Buchenwald, we then were-- were sent to-- did you want to ask something?
No.
We-- yeah, we were separated from the Jews.
Naturally, in Buchenwald, you had the barracks.
There were-- everybody had a certain barracks, a number assigned of barracks.
There were 100 barracks, and there were 100, 200 people in barracks-- 100, 150 people in barracks, because they had the bunk beds and all that.
But we were not really in touch with-- with the Jewish people, because they were, again, separate.
But we knew where they were, because of the stars, because they had the yellow star.
And on the stars was written, Jude.
We-- we got the little red triangle.
And in the triangle, they would make an initial in it from which country you came from.
Like we came from France.
The people who came from Poland had a P in it.
The people who came from-- from Czechoslovakia had a T in it.
Then the gypsy had the-- had-- had G in it.
The-- and then they had the homosexuals.
They the pink ones-- triangle.
And then they had the German criminals, also in camps, because they emptied the prisons to help them-- help them work, and let them-- give them more freedom in the camps.
So they had the green-- green triangle.
And then, they had the ones who were, what you call, deserter or-- or communist.
They had the black.
So everybody had a different identification, so that they know where you come from, and what you are, who you are.
Yeah.
We were treated a little different, because they need-- they wanted us-- they wanted to use us, more or less, as workers.
They didn't want to-- they didn't want to destroy us, because they needed workers in different camps, or in different factories.
Because, you know, they had so many men in the fronts and all, during the war.
They needed helpers.
They didn't need-- they couldn't destroy everybody, at that time.
They would have destroyed us later, but not at the-- at-- at that time.
After being in Buchenwald for three-- three weeks-- four-- for three to four weeks-- we were shipped again with 500 people to Peenemunde.
Peenemunde is in the-- the North Sea.
It's where they manufactured the V-2.
First, they manufactured the V-1.
That was the little-- like a little airplane-- airplane bomb.
Then there became the V-2.
That became the real bomb-- the real-- what do you call?
The V-2 rockets?
The rocket.
That was the rocket.
Like we see now, the rockets.
So but when we came to Peenemunde, the Wernher Von Braun was the starter of the rocket--
--program.
Very intelligent man.
Very smart man.
But he started the rocket program.
In Peenemunde.
That was his lovely home-- area, because it was free, and the sea, and everything, you know?
He could do what-- what he wanted there.
And Hitler gave him freedom there.
Again-- again, the same thing-- in the train, in these boxcars.
And so many people in there.
And-- and-- they had no other transportation.
There was no other transportation.
So that was in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June, July of 1943.
OK?
When we came to Peenemunde, we started to work in the-- they had just built a new factory of-- for manufacturing the V-2.
We started working on assembling the machineries.
I was called to help on the camouflage.
On the top of the roof, there was a big, big factory, so we had to lay all kind of wires.
And then we had to put green-- green stuff on there.
And then we had to lay brown, for making look-- look like it is a road, you now, twirling around, and all kind of stuff.
So I was working with a-- with 12 men on top of the roof.
And one of the guy said, you're going to be in charge.
And you know, after working so many hours-- and it was hot out there-- we-- I took a little rest.
Again, they had an informer.
They always had somebody who was an-- was there to inform the Germans.
About 10, 15 minutes later, the SS comes up, comes close to me.
He said, you was resting and sleeping.
I said-- I didn't-- didn't answer, so he took down my number.
And we went down.
At 5 o'clock, the horn blow.
And everybody goes down and goes to eat the soup with the peels-- peels of the potatoes and whatever they had-- rutabaga-- whatever they had, they gave us to eat.
And then we had to have an assembly.
They assembled--
Into the-- they assembled everybody.
Every night, they would count if everybody was there from the camp.
They would call it an assembly.
Mm-hm.
An appel, or-- yeah.
Mm-hm.
But they called it the assembly.
Mm-hm.
So we-- they counted everyone.
And if somebody was missing, we had to wait until they found that person.
But once they counted everybody and everybody was accounted for, suddenly, my number came up again.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
That, it stayed always with me.
Yeah, it stayed always with me, because we still belonged to the Camp Buchenwald.
My number was called.
And I knew what was going to come-- come up for now.
So they took us inside, into the barracks.
And in the barracks, there was a assembly.
Our-- we actually slept underneath the factory.
The factory was built up.
And then we-- they had the-- the camp was underneath the factory.
So we were-- my name was called and-- my number was called, and I had to get in front of everybody.
And suddenly, somebody came with a bench and a big stick.
So I had to lay down on the bench and got 25 slashes.
Was this somebody-- did he have a uniform on?
The-- who gave it?
Yes.
Yes.
A German.
A German.
A German.
OK?
So but, I-- I outsmarted him a little bit, I have to say that.
Before we went onto the roll call, we all had towels to wash ourself.
I took two towels-- my brother's and mine-- and put it on my hind end, so that nobody could see.
I put it this way down and this way down, so it wouldn't be visible that-- that-- that.
But that doesn't mean much.
I got 25 slashes.
And I-- I bit my teeth.
And I bit my teeth.
I would not dare giving them the satisfaction, really, to scream.
Because the more you screamed, the harder they would hit.
So I-- I screamed somehow, but not really a outburst scream, but I screamed.
Well, I had my 25 slashes.
And I tell you, I could hardly walk away from that bench.
I was thinking they're going to kill me.
That's what I was thinking of, because, you know, what else can you think about?
I got-- I got those slashes.
And then we went to bed.
I didn't know which way to lay, and which way to sit, and which way to do what.
And I was in the upper bunk.
And I said, whoever sleeps down there, I'm going to stay down here.
I can't climb up there.
It was my brother, anyway, so-- so I laid down in the-- in the-- in the-- in the bunk bed and suffered.
And what else could you do?
On the same night, the British-- British Air Force bombed us.
They-- they-- they knew about Camp-- Camp Peenemunde.
And we were bombed all around our camps and all over.
Just one bomb came close to our sleeping quarter.
I don't know how they knew that we were in there, but somebody knew exactly.
We have to-- I have to say, we had some engineers who communicated with England.
They made themself little SOS computers, Like you're sending out-- sending out--
Information.
Well, those things, we knew about our-- amongst our French comrades, you know, the-- the-- that something was going on.
Our-- our conversation was always, I hope we come out.
And we're going to do as much damage we can do, that they lose the war.
That was all our thinking.
And we used to talk about our youth, how-- you know, how we grew up, and what we did, and things like this.
But there was not much.
Once a while, I saw a German paper.
I read the German paper, and then I would pass it on to my French comrades, you know?
Hitler was doing this, and this, and that.
So we were bombed out in Peenemunde.
And became-- we went then to assemble everything, the equipment.
Put it on-- on-- on flat cars, so that it would be transported to another factory.
When we were finished with that, we went back to Buchenwald.
Before we go to Buchenwald, how was Peenemunde-- the living conditions in Peenemunde, how were they different from Buchenwald?
Peenemunde was-- was-- was pretty good, because, you know, they needed us as workers.
They didn't treat us as slaves.
So they-- they did treat us a little better in Peenemunde, in order of food and things, but nothing otherwise.
Just the food was a little better.
But we all had to be workers.
We still were prisoners.
It didn't matter, except it was cleaner, because it was a clean factory.
And the bunk bed was clean.
Everything was-- was-- was neat, you know?
Not real neat, but what looks-- looked cleaner.
Yeah.
